gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Bacillus subtilis J46, isolated from fermented soybeans, grows poorly on galactose (specific growth rate ~0.03 h⁻¹). Galactose is a significant carbon source in soybean biomass, so improving its utilization could enhance fermentation productivity. The goal is to use adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) to evolve J46 for faster growth on galactose, identify causal mutations, and validate them via reverse engineering.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: ALE of B. subtilis J46 on galactose minimal medium for ~5000 generations increased specific growth rate from 0.03 to 0.319 h⁻¹. Evolved strain BSGA14 had 63 SNPs; two key mutations in araR (H226R) and glcR (frameshift) were identified. Reverse-engineered strain BSGALE4 (araR H226R + ΔglcR) achieved similar growth (0.296 h⁻¹). Evolved strains showed higher protease and β-galactosidase in mock soybean medium.
process_signals: Initial growth defect on galactose (0.03 h⁻¹)
ALE improved growth tenfold
Two mutations account for most fitness gain
Reverse engineering confirmed causality
Application test in soybean medium showed enhanced enzyme production

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: B. subtilis is a GRAS industrial workhorse for enzyme production and food fermentation, often using soybean biomass. Galactose is a major component of soybean biomass but its utilization by B. subtilis is poorly studied. ALE is a proven method to improve microbial phenotypes under selective pressure. This study aims to enhance galactose utilization in B. subtilis J46 via ALE and uncover genetic changes.
process_signals: Motivation: soybean biomass contains galactose, but B. subtilis J46 grows poorly on it
Hypothesis: ALE can improve galactose utilization
Goal: identify genetic basis of adaptation

section_gists.heading: Materials and methods
section_gists.gist: Section header only; no process details.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution and strain isolation
section_gists.gist: ALE was performed in triplicate in M9 minimal medium with 2% galactose at 37°C, 200 rpm. Initially, 0.11% yeast extract was added to boost growth, then gradually reduced to zero. Cultures transferred every 24h, starting OD600 0.05. After ALE, single clones isolated on M9 galactose agar. The fastest-growing clone was named BSGA14.
process_signals: Initial supplementation with yeast extract to support growth, then phased out
Serial batch transfers every 24h
Isolation of single clones after evolution

section_gists.heading: Analytical methods
section_gists.gist: Cell growth measured by OD600. Galactose concentration determined by HPLC with Aminex HPX-87H column and RI detector.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Section header only; no process details.

section_gists.heading: for galactose utilization in a minimal medium
section_gists.gist: ALE of J46 on galactose minimal medium. Initially 1% yeast extract added, removed after 1000 generations. Specific growth rate increased from 0.03 to 0.319 h⁻¹ over ~5000 generations, with major improvement between 1000-2500 generations. Evolved strain BSGA14 reached OD600 6.96 vs 0.67 for wild type in 16h.
process_signals: Initial growth rate very low (0.03 h⁻¹)
Yeast extract supplementation needed initially
Growth rate improved tenfold
Major fitness increase occurred between generations 1000-2500
BSGA14 isolated as fastest grower
methods: ALE with gradual removal of yeast extract

section_gists.heading: Genomic resequencing of strains BSGA14
section_gists.gist: Whole-genome resequencing of BSGA14 vs J46 revealed 63 SNPs. Two key mutations: araR H226R (missense) and glcR 243InsA (frameshift). These mutations increased in frequency from 0 to >90% during ALE, coinciding with growth rate increase. Other mutations had low frequencies or no trend.
process_signals: 63 total SNPs found
Focus on transcription factors in carbon catabolism
araR and glcR mutations swept to high frequency
No mutations in galactose metabolic pathway genes
Mutation frequency tracked over generations

section_gists.heading: Mutation on araR increases galactose utilization via enhancing galactose uptake
section_gists.gist: araR encodes a repressor of arabinose metabolism; araE transporter has broad specificity including galactose. araR H226R likely reduces DNA binding, derepressing araE. Reverse-engineered strain BSGALE1 (araR H226R) grew faster (0.214 h⁻¹) and showed upregulation of araE, araB, abnA. Deletion of araR (BSGALE2) gave lower growth (0.170 h⁻¹), indicating H226R is not a simple loss-of-function.
process_signals: Hypothesis: araR H226R derepresses araE, increasing galactose uptake
BSGALE1 confirmed improved growth and gene expression
BSGALE2 (ΔaraR) grew slower than BSGALE1, suggesting H226R has a specific effect (possibly impaired dimerization)
failures_or_limits: ΔaraR did not fully recapitulate H226R phenotype, indicating complex regulation

section_gists.heading: Effect of the nonsense-mutation in glcR induces acceleration of phosphosugar catabolic reaction
section_gists.gist: glcR is a transcriptional repressor involved in carbon catabolite repression and sugar phosphorylation. Deletion of glcR (BSGALE3) increased growth rate to 0.216 h⁻¹ and upregulated Leloir pathway genes (galE, galT, galK). Double mutant BSGALE4 (araR H226R + ΔglcR) achieved growth rate of 0.296 h⁻¹, close to BSGA14.
process_signals: glcR mutation likely enhances phosphosugar metabolism
BSGALE3 showed improved growth and Leloir pathway upregulation
Combining araR H226R and ΔglcR recapitulated most of the evolved phenotype

section_gists.heading: Soybean broth utilization in BSGA14 and BSGALE4 strain
section_gists.gist: In mock soybean biomass medium with galactose and glucose, J46 showed incomplete galactose consumption and low growth (OD<4). BSGA14 and BSGALE4 reached OD>10, with near-complete sugar utilization. BSGALE4 produced 20.5 U/mL protease and 65.4 U/mL β-galactosidase, higher than J46.
process_signals: Evolved and engineered strains outperformed wild type in soybean medium
BSGA14 had 5.47-fold higher biomass than J46 at 24h
BSGALE4 had 3.08-fold higher biomass
Enhanced enzyme production observed
methods: Cultivation in mock soybean hydrolysis medium

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: ALE successfully improved galactose utilization. araR and glcR mutations accounted for >80% of fitness gain. Transcription factor mutations are common in ALE. araR H226R likely affects dimerization, not simple loss-of-function. glcR deletion enhances phosphosugar metabolism. Study demonstrates potential for industrial application.
process_signals: Two mutations explain most of the phenotype
araR H226R is not equivalent to deletion
glcR role in CCR and sugar phosphorylation
ALE is effective for metabolic engineering

section_gists.heading: Availability of data and materials
section_gists.gist: Genome sequences deposited under BioProject PRJNA1028132 (J46) and PRJNA1028133 (BSGA14).

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Initial problem: B. subtilis J46 grows very slowly on galactose, limiting its use on soybean biomass.
Hypothesis: ALE with galactose as sole carbon source will select for mutations that improve galactose utilization.
Experimental design: Serial batch culture in M9 + galactose, initially with yeast extract, then without, for ~5000 generations.
Observation: Growth rate increased tenfold; major improvement between 1000-2500 generations.
Genomic analysis: 63 SNPs found; two in transcriptional regulators (araR, glcR) swept to high frequency.
Mechanistic hypothesis: araR H226R derepresses araE, increasing galactose uptake; glcR frameshift enhances phosphosugar metabolism.
Validation: Reverse engineering single and double mutants confirmed causality; double mutant recapitulated ~93% of evolved growth rate.
Application: Evolved/engineered strains showed improved growth and enzyme production in soybean medium.
Limitations: Other mutations not characterized; industrial scale-up not tested.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the exact mechanism of araR H226R (dimerization defect vs DNA binding)?
Do other 61 SNPs contribute to fitness, and if so, how?
How does glcR deletion specifically upregulate Leloir pathway genes?
What are the performance and stability of evolved strains in large-scale fermentations?
Are there trade-offs in other phenotypes (e.g., growth on other sugars)?

evidence_bank.quote: Efficient utilization of galactose by microorganisms can lead to the production of valuable bio-products and improved metabolic processes.
While Bacillus subtilis has inherent pathways for galactose metabolism, there is potential for enhancement via evolutionary strategies.
This study aimed to boost galactose utilization in B. subtilis using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) and to elucidate the genetic and metabolic changes underlying the observed enhancements.

Initially, 0.11 percent yeast extract was added to the minimal medium to boost the growth of J46. By progressively lowering the supplementation, growth without supplementation was eventually attained.
Batch cultures were manually transferred to fresh medium every 24 h.
The strains of B. subtilis underwent multiple rounds of adaptive laboratory evolution (approximately 5000 generations) in an environment that favored the use of galactose.
Upon selecting the evolved strain BSGA14, a comprehensive whole-genome sequencing revealed the presence of 63 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs).
Two of them, located in the coding sequences of the genes araR and glcR, were found to be the advantageous mutations after reverse engineering.
1% yeast extract was initially added to M9 minimal medium containing 2% (w/v) galactose. After 1000 generations, cultivation was carried out without the addition of yeast extract.
The significant increase in fitness on galactose was confirmed between 1000 and 2500 generations of ALE.
A total of 63 SNP mutations were identified in the BSGA14 strain
a significant increment was recorded between the 1000 to 2000 generation span, which aligned with the escalation of the specific growth rate
The expression of araE (increased by 3.99-fold and 2.88-fold), araB (increased by 3.45-fold and 2.87-fold), and abnA (increased by 2.99-fold and 2.65-fold) was much higher in BSGA14 and BSGALE1
Reverse engineering of B. subtilis J46 introduced mutation of $araR^{H226R}$

canonical_events.summary: B. subtilis J46 grows poorly on galactose, limiting its use on soybean biomass.
ALE can enhance galactose utilization in B. subtilis.
ALE performed in M9 minimal medium with 2% galactose, initially supplemented with yeast extract, then phased out.
ALE resulted in evolved strain BSGA14 with specific growth rate of 0.319 h⁻¹, a tenfold increase from wild-type 0.03 h⁻¹.
Whole-genome resequencing of BSGA14 revealed 63 SNPs, with key mutations in araR (H226R) and glcR (frameshift).
Mutation frequencies of araR and glcR surged from 0 to >90% between generations 1000-2000, coinciding with growth rate increase.
Hypothesis that araR H226R reduces DNA binding affinity, derepressing araE and improving galactose uptake.
Reverse-engineered single mutant BSGALE1 (araR H226R) showed improved growth (0.214 h⁻¹) and upregulation of araE, araB, abnA.
Unexpected result: araR deletion strain BSGALE2 grew slower than araR H226R mutant, indicating complex regulation.
Reverse engineering of glcR deletion (BSGALE3) and combined araR H226R + ΔglcR (BSGALE4) mutations, growth profiling.
Gene expression analysis of galactose metabolic pathway in BSGA14 and BSGALE3 showed upregulation of galE, galT, galK.
Double mutant BSGALE4 achieved growth rate of 0.296 h⁻¹, close to BSGA14, confirming araR and glcR mutations account for >80% of fitness gain.
Evolved strain BSGA14 and engineered BSGALE4 showed improved growth and enzyme production in mock soybean medium.

findings.problem: Step 3 claims specific growth rate numbers (0.319 ± 0.005 h⁻¹, 0.03 ± 0.008 h⁻¹) that are not directly supported by the provided evidence. The evidence only mentions 'significant increase in fitness' and 'approximately 5000 generations', but no exact growth rates.
findings.revision_instruction: Either add evidence that explicitly states the growth rates (e.g., from a table or figure caption) or remove the unsupported numbers and describe the increase qualitatively.

findings.problem: Step 7 claims a specific growth rate of 0.214 h⁻¹ for BSGALE1, but the provided evidence does not include this number. The evidence only mentions fold changes in gene expression and that growth profiles were measured.
findings.revision_instruction: Include evidence that explicitly states the growth rate of BSGALE1, or remove the unsupported number and describe the growth improvement qualitatively.

findings.problem: Step 11 claims a specific growth rate of 0.296 h⁻¹ for BSGALE4, but the provided evidence does not include this number. The evidence only mentions that araR and glcR mutations account for >80% of fitness improvements and that growth profiles were measured.
findings.revision_instruction: Include evidence that explicitly states the growth rate of BSGALE4, or remove the unsupported number and describe the growth improvement qualitatively.

findings.problem: Step 5 mentions 'between generations 1000-2000', but the evidence only says 'between the 1000 to 2000 generation span'. The hyphen might be misinterpreted, but it's essentially the same. However, the step also claims 'surged from 0 to >90%', which is supported by EV_AUTO_001, but the evidence for the generation span is only in EV_C002_E05_02, which does not explicitly state the frequency surge. The combination of evidence is acceptable, but the step could be more precise.
findings.revision_instruction: Ensure that the evidence for the frequency surge and the generation span are clearly linked. Consider adding a note that the frequency surge is from EV_AUTO_001 and the generation span from EV_C002_E05_02.

findings.revision_instruction: Clarify the initial yeast extract concentration. If both were used at different stages, specify. Otherwise, correct to the consistent value from the paper.

findings.problem: Step 12 mentions BSGALE4 showed improved biomass, but the evidence provided (EV_C003_E05_01, EV_C003_E05_02, EV_C004_E06_01) only mentions enzyme activities for BSGA14 and J46, and EV_C004_E06_01 has a different set of numbers (protease 20.5 ± 4.8 U/mL, β-galactosidase 65.4 ± 3.5 U/mL) that conflict with EV_C003_E05_01. The step does not provide evidence for BSGALE4 biomass improvement.
findings.revision_instruction: Either add evidence for BSGALE4 biomass improvement or remove the claim. Also, resolve the conflicting enzyme activity numbers between EV_C003_E05_01 and EV_C004_E06_01.

the wild type strain J46 demonstrated substantially lower enzyme activities, with protease and $\beta$ -galactosidase activities recorded at $3.4 \pm 0.6$ U/mL and $1.3 \pm 0.05$ U/mL, respectively

validation_methods: Reverse engineering of single and double mutants
Growth profiling in minimal and soybean media
success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE successfully enhanced galactose utilization in B. subtilis J46, yielding strain BSGA14 with tenfold growth increase. Key mutations araR H226R and glcR frameshift account for >80% of fitness gain, validated by reverse engineering. Evolved and engineered strains show improved enzyme production in soybean medium.
limitations: Other mutations beyond araR and glcR may contribute to fitness
Scale-up and industrial application not tested
Mechanism of glcR contribution not fully elucidated
